segments.text:
Section 6.5
NUCLEOPHILES AND NUCLEOPHILICITY
TABLE 6.2 Nucleophilicity of Some Common Nucleophiles
Reactivity class | Nucleophile | Relative reactivity*
Very good nucleophiles | I−, HS−, RS− | >105
Good nucleophiles | Br−, HO−, RO−, CN−, N3− | 104
Fair nucleophiles | NH3, Cl−, F−, RCO2− | 103
Weak nucleophiles | H2O, ROH | 1
Very weak nucleophiles | RCO2H | 10−2
*Relative reactivity is k (nucleophile)/ k (methanol) for typical S N 2 reactions and is approximate. Data pertain to methanol as the solvent .

Solvolysis
Because the nucleophile in S N 1 is typically weak and liquid, it can be used as solvent in a solvolysis reaction
Common when water, ethanol, or methanol is the nucleophile
Ensures that as soon as RX dissociates, the carbocation is captured by a nucleophilic molecule
Solvolytic conditions can also be used for S N 2 with weak nucleophiles

Nucleophiles & Nucleophilicity
Nucleophiles are “nucleus loving.” The most common type of nucleophile is a lone pair on a negative or neutral atom
Nucleophilicity is a measure of the speed (kinetics!) with which a nucleophile reacts
Nucleophilicity is related to basicity, with some quirks

Trends in Nucleophilicity
Across a row the periodic table, nucleophilicity tracks with basicity
CH 3 − > NH 2 − > OH − > F −
Down a group, nucleophilicity increases even though basicity decreases!
Larger and more polarizable anions are more nucleophilic but less basic
Solvation also plays a role:
Smaller anions are more easily solvated and shielded from acting as nucleophiles
Larger anions are more difficult to solvate and more “available” to act as nucleophiles
Ultimately, carbon is not hydrogen .

Trends in Nucleophilicity-1
TABLE 6.2 Nucleophilicity of Some Common Nucleophiles
Reactivity class | Nucleophile | Relative reactivity*
Very good nucleophiles | I−, HS−, RS− | >105
Good nucleophiles | Br−, HO−, RO−, CN−, N3− | 104
Fair nucleophiles | NH3, Cl−, F−, RCO2− | 103
Weak nucleophiles | H2O, ROH | 1
Very weak nucleophiles | RCO2H | 10−2
*Relative reactivity is k (nucleophile)/ k (methanol) for typical S N 2 reactions and is approximate. Data pertain to methanol as the solvent .

Section 6.6
THE S N 1 MECHANISM OF NUCLEOPHILIC SUBSTITUTION

Kinetics of S N 1
Substitution of tertiary alcohols and alkyl halides is found to be unimolecular
The rate law depends only on the concentration of the electrophile
Only the electrophile is involved in the rate-determining transition state
In this TS, the leaving group is separating from the electrophilic carbon and taking a pair of electrons with it

Reaction Coordinate Diagram
Loss of leaving group
Attack of weak nucleophile
Loss of proton

Section 6.7
STEREOCHEMISTRY OF S N 1 REACTIONS

Planar Intermediate of S N 1
The carbocation intermediate of S N 1 is achiral as long as no other chirality centers are present
The positive carbon and three attached atoms form a plane
Scrambling is expected .
In theory, the nucleophile should attack either side of a planar achiral cation with equal probability, affording a racemic mixture

Practical S N 1 : Partial Inversion
Many S N 1 reactions of optically active starting materials do not yield racemates!
Mostly inversion occurs, suggesting that the leaving group “hovers” near the cationic carbon during nucleophilic attack
The cationic carbon and leaving group anion form a tight ion pair .

Section 6.8
CARBOCATION REARRANGEMENTS IN S N 1 REACTIONS

Carbocation Rearrangements
Some S N 1 reactions, especially of secondary alkyl halides, give unexpected products

Carbocation Rearrangements-1
The alkyl halide ionizes to give a carbocation and bromide ion. This is the rate-determining step.
The intermediate carbocation rapidly rearranges to form a more stable carbocation
Keep in mind that (a) the product cation must be more stable than the reactant cation and (b) only adjacent groups can migrate .

Carbocation Rearrangements-2
The tertiary carbocation is attacked by water acting as a nucleophile.
Proton transfer from the alkyloxonium ion to water completes the process .
